label: Formula 4.13a
section: RELATIVE POWER OF RECOMBINATION AND GENETIC DRIFT
nearby_text: re largely driven by mutation, recombination, and genetic drift, and the population is in equilibrium, by using expressions from Ohta and Kimura (1969b) for the two-allele model, it can be shown that $$ E(\Delta_{L})\simeq\frac{\theta(1+2\theta)(18+\rho_{L})}{2(1+\theta)A} $$ (4.13a) where $$ A=9+6.5\rho_{L}+0.5\rho_{L}^{2}+19\theta\rho_{L}+12\theta^{2}\rho_{L}+\theta\rho_{L}^{2}+54\theta+80\theta^{2}+32\theta^{3} $$ (4.13b)

label: Formula 4.19
section: EFFECTIVE POPULATION SIZE
nearby_text: so the utility of the overall approach becomes diminishingly small in populations with large effective sizes. Assuming equal sample sizes for each locus, the sampling variance of $ \widehat{N}_e $ is $$ \mathrm{Var}(\widehat{N}_{e})\simeq\left(\frac{8N_{e}^{4}}{t^{2}M}\right)\left(\frac{1}{4N_{e}^{2}}+\frac{1}{N_{e}t\tilde{n}}+\frac{1}{t^{2}\tilde{n}^{2}}\right) $$ (4.19) where M denotes the number of independent allelic comparisons and $ \tilde{n} $ is the harmonic mean of the per-locus sample sizes in the two generations (Pollak 1983). label: Formula 4.22c
section: MUTATION RATE
nearby_text: nd $ N_t $ are the total numbers of cells in the culture at times 0 and t, so long as the observed mutant frequencies are < 0.1, so that $ \ln(1 - f) \simeq -f $, Equation 4.22b further simplifies to $$ \widehat{u}_{o}\simeq\frac{f_{t}-f_{0}}{\ln(N_{t}/N_{0})} $$ (4.22c) which is simply the rate of accumulation of observable mutations per cell division.

  "nearby_text": "$ per gene with a reduction in fitness equal to $ h_s $ per induced mutation, the selective disadvantage of the mutator allele induced by linkage disequilibrium with this particular fitness locus is $$ s_{d}\\simeq\\frac{h s\\cdot\\Delta u}{1-(1-h s)(1-c)} $$ (4.23a) assuming $ \\Delta u \\ll h s $ (Kimura 1967; Dawson 1999). An intuitive feeling for the structure of this equation can be obtained by noting that $ h s $ is the rate of removal of a deleterious mutati"

label: Formula 4.23b
section: MUTATION RATE
nearby_text: so the selective disadvantage at equilibrium is simply the ratio of the rates of gain and loss of associated fitness load per generation. For unlinked loci ( $ c = 0.5 $), this expression reduces to $$ s_{d}\simeq\frac{2h s\cdot\Delta u}{1+h s} $$ (4.23b) whereas in the absence of recombination $ (c = 0.0) $, $$ s_{d}=\Delta u $$ (4.23c)

section: MUTATION RATE
nearby_text: in the deleterious mutation rate of $ \Delta U $, after accounting for the spatial distribution of random mutations, the total induced selection coefficient against the mutator allele is found to be $$ s_{d,T}\simeq\frac{2h s\cdot\Delta U(L-1+\phi)}{L(1+h s)} $$ (4.24a) where $$ \phi=1+\ln\left(\frac{1+hs-(1-hs)e^{-1}}{2hs}\right) $$ (4.24b) is the approximate elevation in the average induced fitness effect of mutations on the mutator-bearing chromosome relative to
